coredata.dc:title: The fluorescence properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave green approaches
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Chunxiao
Tian, Chunlan
Zhang, Meng
Li, Fengyan
Li, Yuzhao
Zhang, Fan
Zhang, Jin
Chen, Guo
Tang, Ju
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have exceptional fluorescence performance and a broad spectrum of applications. Everyone knows that heteroatom doping is advantageous for regulating electronic states and transition channels in carbon dot systems. Thus, CDs exhibit different properties under different experimental methods or doped conditions. To clarify the role of extrinsic factors, doped carbon dots with excellent fluorescence properties are synthesized in this paper, and the effects of pH level and microwave heating time on doped CDs' photoluminescence (PL) are thoroughly investigated. Utilizing a pyrolysis mixture of Ascorbic acid and NH3H2O, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) linked by N elements are synthesized. The impact of surface functions on these nanomaterials' optical characteristics is examined using XPS and PL spectroscopic techniques. The research found that when the duration of microwave heating increases, the tiny molecules become too dehydrated and carbonized at elevated temperatures. Simultaneously, reducing oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of carbon dots causes the weakening of fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, with the increase of pH value, the accumulation of excessive -OH in the solution leads to the fluorescence weakening of NCDs, leading to a steady rise and then a decrease in the luminescence intensity. This research has particular scientific significance for the fluorescence regulation of NCDs and its application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Microwave heating
Wavelength regulation of fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TEM, XRD, FT-IR, Raman analysis of NCDs
The UV–vis absorption spectrum, PL, and XPS energy spectrum
Analysis of the luminescence mechanism of NCDs and its sensitivity mechanism to pH value

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic strategy for the preparation of NCDs.
TEM micrograph (a), average size distribution (b)) and lattice fringes (HRTEM image (c)) of as-synthesized NCDs. XRD diffractogram (d), Raman spectra (e), FT-IR spectra (f)of NCDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and PL spectra (b) of NCDs with different microwave heating durations. The inset in (a) is the chromaticity diagrams of NCDs under different microwave heating durations. (c) and (e) show the full-scan XPS spectra of NCDs prepared by microwave heating for 120 min and 180 min, respectively. The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of NCDs are shown respectively in (d) and (f).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra(a) and PL spectra(b) of NCDs with different pH values. The inset is the chromaticity diagrams of NCDs with different pH values. (c) and (e) show the full-scan XPS spectra of NCDs in NaOH solutions with pH=8 and 11, respectively. The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of NCDs are shown respectively in(d) and (f).

(a) Tauc curves of NCD under different microwave heating times. (b) Tauc curves of NCD at different pH values. (c) Luminescence mechanism diagram of NCDs . (d) Structural diagram of NCDs dispersed in deionized water. (e) Structural diagram of NCDs dispersed in sodium hydroxide.
The percentage composition of different functional groups in the NCDs sample determined by C1s XPS analysis.

row.entry: The percentage compositions of components in C1s spectra

row.entry: The percentage compositions of components in C1s spectra

head.ce:title: The fluorescence properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave green approaches

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kunming Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have exceptional fluorescence performance and a broad spectrum of applications. Everyone knows that heteroatom doping is advantageous for regulating electronic states and transition channels in carbon dot systems. Thus, CDs exhibit different properties under different experimental methods or doped conditions. To clarify the role of extrinsic factors, doped carbon dots with excellent fluorescence properties are synthesized in this paper, and the effects of pH level and microwave heating time on doped CDs' photoluminescence (PL) are thoroughly investigated. Utilizing a pyrolysis mixture of Ascorbic acid and NH3H2O, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) linked by N elements are synthesized. The impact of surface functions on these nanomaterials' optical characteristics is examined using XPS and PL spectroscopic techniques. The research found that when the duration of microwave heating increases, the tiny molecules become too dehydrated and carbonized at elevated temperatures. Simultaneously, reducing oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of carbon dots causes the weakening of fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, with the increase of pH value, the accumulation of excessive -OH in the solution leads to the fluorescence weakening of NCDs, leading to a steady rise and then a decrease in the luminescence intensity. This research has particular scientific significance for the fluorescence regulation of NCDs and its application.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional materials with a special kind of "core-shell" nanostructure [1,2]. As far as the chemical structure is concerned, CDs are generally composed of sp 2/sp 3 hybridized carbon, nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups, and post-modified functional groups, which are flaky or spherical and have a size of about 2–10 nm [3,4]. This means that CDs have outstanding photophysical characteristics and extensive practical applications in many domains, such as photocatalysis, biomedical, probe detection, photothermal therapy, light-emitting devices, and electrochemical energy storage [5,6]. Furthermore, CDs and metal quantum dots have similar properties. Still, metal quantum dots are usually composed of group III-V or II-VI elements, while CDs do not contain heavy metal elements and thus have very low biotoxicity. Therefore, CDs are considered a potential, non-toxic substitute for semiconductor quantum dots [7,8].

Furthermore, researchers have prepared CDs with different sizes and edge/surface functional groups using preparation methods, expanding the properties and applications of various CD types. However, there are still a lot of difficulties in preparing CDs with high yield and excellent optical properties simultaneously and realizing specific applications. In particular, (i) CDs preparation and the design of surface/edge structures have always been significant challenges. The surface/edge state directly determines CDs' boundary and quantum confinement effects, affecting their exceptional physicochemical features. Thus, the CDs' edge structure design will directly determine their applications' scope [9,10]. (ii) Utilizing easily adjustable experimental conditions to prepare CDs with different sizes and shapes is also a significant challenge. The reason is that as the size of CDs changes, especially when the size gets smaller and smaller, the above two effects will be more prominent, and their properties will be more excellent. Therefore, realizing a good grasp of the size of CDs is essential for improving its properties [11–13]. (iii) the study of CDs' characteristics and uses needs to be extended. The mechanism of its edge structure, size, and the number of specific layers on its properties still need to be studied in depth [14,15]. Therefore, there is a need for a more in-depth study of the surface/nuclear structure-property correlation of CDs further to comprehend the mechanism of CDs' fluorescence sensing, realize their excellent properties, and expand their practical applications.

Currently, the traditional preparation methods for CDs are broadly categorized under two main types, i.e., "top-down" and "bottom-up." [16,17]. This so-called "top-down" method implicates stripping/cutting large-sized carbon materials into nanodots of less than 20 nm, which can be interpreted as making them smaller [18–21]. The "bottom-up" method uses small molecules, such as amino acids and citric acid, as base material to synthesize CDs, and these methods mainly include high-temperature pyrolysis [22], hydrothermal method [23], and microwave-assisted method [24].

Research on CD applications and preparation techniques has long been in demand. Overall, edge/surface modulation, preparation parameter settings, and synthesis method all affect the PL characteristics of CDs [25]. More precisely, the frequency and strength of PL emission are limited by the interactions between the different functional groups or edge states, the electronic forms of the conjugated domains of sp 2, the interactions between chemical groups/hanging bonds in the surface conditions, and the characters of the fluorophore [3,25]. Previous studies have also shown a close link between the fluorescence properties of carbon-based nanomaterials and their surface states [26].

The microwave method is widely used in these preparative methods due to its rapid and homogeneous heating, simple and convenient operation, and wide range of applicable carbon sources [27,28]. The fluorescence behavior of carbon dots can be altered by surface functionalization and heteroatom doping. Heteroatom doping is a widely used strategy to increase the quantum yield of CDs. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs can be significantly enhanced by utilizing the heteroatom doping technique, and therefore, heteroatoms are frequently doped in the synthesis of CDs [29,30]. Among them, nitrogen doping or co-doping of nitrogen with other atoms is considered to be the most effective method. Doping of nitrogen atoms can change CDs' internal structure or surface state, thereby modulating their energy gap width and electron density and thus increasing their quantum yields [31–33]. In addition, most of the CDs show only one intense fluorescent color under UV excitation. Nitrogen doping in CDs can not only regulate the excitation wavelength of CDs but also effectively modulate the intrinsic properties of CDs and improve the optical properties of CDs, which makes CDs show different bright colors [34]. Before this, the effect of CDs edge functional group modulation on fluorescence has also been investigated [3], providing a functional experimental approach to enhance luminous CDs' optical characteristics. The main incentive for the research was the role of clarifying extrinsic factors and synthesizing doped CDs with excellent fluorescence properties. Diwan et al. [35] synthesized NCDs using microwave technology, using citric acid and urea as raw materials. The results showed that the quantum yield of the prepared NCDs was as high as 24.69 %. Gao et al. [34] synthesized highly photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using ferric ammonium citrate (FAC) and urea as raw materials by hydrothermal method. By adjusting the excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 510 nm, the NCDs possessed bright blue and green fluorescence, and the fluorescence quantum yield was also high.

Furthermore, the doping mechanism of doped CDs has not yet been comprehensively and systematically studied. For underlying applications of CDs as progressive materials for the optoelectronic technology field, it is crucial and meaningful to explore the fluorescence regulation and performance of doped carbon dots.

This article uses ascorbic acid as the carbon source and ammonia as the nitrogen source to prepare NCDs using microwave heating. Using the controlled variable method, we looked into how varying microwave heating times and pH levels affected the photoluminescence (PL) of NCDs. The size of NCDs was estimated using TEM analysis. UV–Vis and XPS spectroscopy have verified different functional groups attached to the NCDs. This investigation offers a valid experimental approach and technical way to adjust the PL wavelength of NCDs and reveal their luminescence mechanism.

This paper chose vitamin C and ammonia to prepare NCDs. Vitamin C (C6H8O6), also known as ascorbic acid, presents a colorless crystalline form, unstable and easily soluble in water, and is stable in alkaline environments. When the aqueous solution is pH<7, it is effortless to be destroyed by intense light, alkaline substances, high temperature, and oxygen to destroy its structure. It is highly reductive and easily oxidized [36–38]. Since ammonia solution pH=8, this paper chose ammonia as the nitrogen doping reagent.

More specifically, the NCDs are prepared in the following ways: (i) Vitamin C was homogeneously mixed with ammonia reagent in a mass ratio of 12:1. The mixture is placed in a beaker and subjected to ultrasonic vibration for 5 min. (ii) The beaker containing the mixture was put into a microwave for heating at a temperature of 120 °C, and the specimen was removed and distributed in deionized water after heating for a certain length of time. (iii) The mixture is stirred magnetically for 10 min so the substance is evenly and thoroughly mixed with water after standing for 30 min. (iv) A 30-minute centrifugation at 12,000 rpm is performed on the upper layer of the clear liquid. Until the end of the centrifugation to obtain the NCDs solution, Fig. 1 exhibits the process of preparation.

Adjusting pH by changing the concentration of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution and deionized water. 12 g of vitamin C was used as the carbon source and heated at a constant temperature of 15 min at 300 °C to obtain the carbons. Appropriate amounts of NaOH at pH=8, 9, 10, 11, and deionized water were added as the modifiers and stirred well using a stirrer. After 30 min of standing, the upper layer of the clear liquid was centrifuged in a centrifuge at 12,000 r/d for 20 min. When centrifugation was finished, the upper layer of the clear liquid was taken to get the solution of the NCDs in diverse alkaline environments.

This study observed NCDs morphology using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the elementary composition of samples. The UV–visible spectrophotometer (Specord200, Germany) was used to measure the absorption spectra of NCD. The PL emissions were observed at room temperature using traditional PL equipment (IHR320, Jobin Yvon, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, XRD, FT-IR, Raman analysis of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2(a) shows the surface morphology of the NCDs. These pictures demonstrated these NCDs are well-dispersed. The size of NCDs forms a quasi-normal distribution, and the average diameter is 5.51 nm, see Fig. 2(b), which was determined by statistical analysis for over 200 NCDs particles using the Image J program. Fig. 2(c) shows that the lattice stripes of NCDs are well-defined, and the lattice spacing is about 0.223 nm, similar to the (111) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the crystalline nature of NCDs [39,40]. Fig. 2(d) shows the XRD spectra of the NCDs. Due to the high degree of disorder of the carbon atoms, a broad diffraction peak appears at the position of the characteristic 2θ peak at 28.24°, indicating the formation of a crystalline structure in the carbon structure of the NCDs, which is in agreement with the above TEM results.

In addition, Raman spectra are a powerful tool for characterizing the chemical state of carbon in NCDs. As can be seen in Fig. 2(e), the Raman spectra of NCDs show two distinct characteristic peaks located at 1224 cm−1 (D band) and 1695 cm−1 (G band), respectively. Usually, the D and G bands' integrated intensities (ID/IG) are used to compare the structural order between crystalline and amorphous graphite systems [41]. The results show that the ID/IG of NCDs is 0.21, indicating that the doping of N in the samples produces a certain amount of structural domains of disordered carbon, thus leading to surface defects in NCDs [42].

The FT-IR spectra of NCDs are shown in Fig. 2(f) to analyze the chemical composition of NCDs. As can be seen from the figure, the absorption peak at 585cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CH bond, the absorption peaks at 1044 cm−1 and 2076 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and C = O [43], and the absorption peaks at 1409 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond and CC/C = C bond, respectively [44]. The absorption peak near 3428 cm−1 is attributed to the OH stretching vibration [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The UV–vis absorption spectrum, PL, and XPS energy spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different microwave heating times and pH values on the photoelectric properties of NCDs samples were studied by absorption and PL spectra. Fig. 3(a) displays the absorption spectra of NCDs at various microwave heating times. We discovered that the CDs exhibit a notable optical absorption at 251 nm in the UV region, and this optically characterized absorption peak is ascribed to π-π* transition with tails extending into the visible region, which is like the results reported by Zheng et al. [46]. Furthermore, the PL spectra in Fig. 3(b) show that the PL emission intensity progressively decreases as the heating duration increases. It can also be observed in the inset of Fig. 3(b) that the PL emission peak position of NCDs is red-shifted with the increase of microwave heating duration at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The inset in Fig. 3(a) displays the chromaticity diagrams of the synthesized NCDs under four different microwave heating times. This chromaticity diagram was used to reflect the PL emission color of NCDs. Under light pumping, the NCDs exhibit blue fluorescence, as can be seen.

To explore the reasons for this phenomenon, this study applies the XPS energy spectra to study the changes in elemental composition and surface functional group content of carbon dot systems. The full-scan XPS energy spectra and C1s high-resolution energy spectra of NCDs prepared under microwave heating are presented in Fig. 3(c–f). The full-scan XPS energy spectra for microwave heating times of 120 and 180 min are shown in Fig. 3(c) and 3(e). The elements percentages of C, O, and N were 72.64 %, 21.93 %, and 5.43 %, respectively, under the heating condition of 120 min. Whereas at the heating time of 180 min, the C, O, and N atomic percentages were 86.74 %, 8.9 %, and 4.35 %, respectively. It is evident from Fig. 3(c) and 3(e) that the main difference between the two samples is in carbon and oxygen content; with the increase of microwave heating duration, the carbon content increased, and the oxygen content decreased. Generally, the degree of carbonization increases with increasing carbon content when the conjugated structure of graphene in the nucleus of carbon dots is prominent. In contrast, the oxygen content decreases, and the number of surface functional groups is reduced. Furthermore, Fig. 3(d) and 3(f) show the C1s high-resolution energy spectra for microwave heating times of 120 min and 180 min, respectively, both of which respond to three characteristic peaks of the reaction, corresponding to the chemical bonds CC/C = C, CO, and OC = O, respectively [47,48]. Based on C1s XPS energy spectra analysis, Table 1 displays the percentage composition of the various functional groups in the NCDs sample. As can be seen from the C1s high-resolution spectra, there is a decrease in the peak position of the CO and OC = O functional groups, which also proves that the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of NCDs has decreased with the increase of the heating time, which affected the PL emission and intensity of the NCDs [49,50].

Thus, the possible reasons for the gradually weakened PL emission intensity as heating time increases are as follows: (i) In the NCDs system, the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface is crucial to the process of PL, which affected the PL emission and intensity of the NCDs. (ii) The findings illustrate that the main distinction between the two samples is the difference in carbon and oxygen content. The intermolecular dehydration aggregation and carbonization process requires a certain amount of time to accumulate. Too short a time cannot be well carbonized, but too long a carbonization time will cause the resulting luminescent clusters to decompose, weakening the fluorescence [46].

In addition, Fig. 4(a) and 4(b) display the absorption and PL spectra of NCDs at various pH conditions. From the figure, it can be found that CDs have a distinct UV absorption peak at 251 nm, and this optically characterized absorption peak is ascribed to π-π *transition with tails extending into the visible region [3], which are like the results reported by Stefanakis et al. [51]. As can be seen from Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots solution showed a tendency of first enhancement and then weakening with the increase of pH at a specific excitation wavelength; the PL intensity of NCDs had an upward enhancement tendency in the between of pH from 8 to 10 with good stability, and when pH=10, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed. The fluorescence intensity was weakened when the pH was 11. The inset in Fig. 4(a) shows the chromaticity diagrams of the synthesized NCDs with different pH values. This chromaticity diagram reflects that the NCDs emit blue PL emission under light pumping.

In addition, NCDs' surface elemental composition and bonding need to be analyzed to understand the surface better. The full-scan XPS energy spectra and C1s energy spectra of NCDs prepared at pH 8 and 11 are given in Fig. 4(e, f). Fig. 4(c) and (e) show the full-scan XPS spectra at pH=8 and 11, respectively. When pH equals 8, the C, O, and N element percentages of NCDs are 75.14 %, 22.23 %, and 2.63 %, respectively. When pH equals 11, the element percentages of C, O, and N and Si of NCDs are 58.07 %, 37.41 %, 2.29 %, and 2.23 %. As seen from (c) and (e), the main difference between the NCDs prepared under different pH conditions lies in carbon and oxygen content differences. With the increase in pH, the carbon content decreases significantly. In contrast, the oxygen content increases significantly, indicating that the alkalinity enhancement of the carbonization degree decreases on the contrary. Meanwhile, the increase of the oxygen content and the increase of the surface functional groups enhance the fluorescence performance of the carbon dots. We also found that when the pH value reached 11, a small amount of Si element appeared in the sample because the instrument detected the substrate during the test, and the test substrate was a silicon wafer. Furthermore, Fig. 4(d) and (f) show the energy spectra of C1s at pH=8 and pH=11, respectively, both of which respond to three fitted peaks corresponding to the chemical bonds CC/C = C, CO, and OC = O, respectively [52,53]. Table 2 shows the percentage composition of the different functional groups in the NCDs sample determined by C1s XPS analysis. As can be observed, the fluorescence peaks of the OC = O functional group are shifted, and the peaks of the functional groups are increased. It also proves that as pH rises, so does the quantity of oxygen-containing functional groups on NCDs' surface, affecting carbon dots' fluorescence emission and intensity. It's easy to ascertain that the main reason for the PL performance of NCDs with various pH is the weakening of fluorescence by excessive -OH [54,55]. Furthermore, the surface of NCDs is rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. When the pH increases, the carboxylic acid on the surface of NCDs sheds hydration, the surface of NCDs is negatively charged, and the solution is mainly the negative ionic form of the NCDs. Thus, the PL intensity is weakened [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the luminescence mechanism of NCDs and its sensitivity mechanism to pH value
ce:section.ce:para: According to the Compton Effect, E = hν, and p = h/λ, the Tauc plot method was employed in this study to determine the bandgap (Eg) of NCDs under various microwave heating times and pH values [3]. As depicted in Fig. 5(a) and (b), a curve of (αhν)n versus hv was plotted, where the linear portion at the edge of absorption bands on these curves was extrapolated to intersect with the horizontal axis, indicating the corresponding Eg value. It can be observed from Fig. 5(a) and (b) that there is a corresponding change in bandgap for NCDs with increasing microwave heating time and pH value, with a concentrated bandgap value around 4.1 eV, and the bandgap size is consistent with conforming to the structural properties of the material, which is the same finding as that of the previous study [3,9]. Fig. 5(c) shows the Jablonski energy level diagram. In the figure, S0, S1, and S2 denote the electronic ground state and the molecule's first and second electronic excited states, respectively. When the molecule absorbs a photon, the electron may jump from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (S1, S2). The excited state electrons are unstable and will return from the excited state (S1, S2) to the ground state (S0) and fluoresce. Of course, it does not always fluoresce and can produce heat or other forms of energy. If the electrons from the excited state (S1) through Intersystem crossing are converted to an electronic T1 excited state, they emit phosphorescence from the excited state T1 back to S0. The difference between the S1 excited state and the T1 excited state is mainly due to the difference in the electron spin direction.

In this study, the pH was adjusted by changing the sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution concentrations and deionized water. The fluorescence response and PL emission characteristics of NCDs in different pH solutions were analyzed. It was found that the changes in the pH sensitivity or PL intensity of NCDs were directly related to the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on their surfaces [57]. As shown in Fig. 5(d), NCDs are mainly composed of three elements, C, N, and O, with nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups at their edges. After NaOH was added for modulation, -COOH on the surface of NCDs was converted to -COONa, and the amount of -COOH decreased [58]. When the pH was increased from 8 to 10, the degree of deprotonation of NCQDs increased, and a higher concentration of -OH was produced at the edge/surface (e.g., Fig. 5(e)), and thus the PL intensity increased [3,58]. NCDs' pH-responsiveness makes them potentially promising for applications such as pH detection.

The influence of microwave heating duration and pH on the PL characteristics of NCDs was researched in this paper, and TEM, UV–Vis, PL, and XPS characterized the NCDs synthesized under various conditions. The experimental outcomes demonstrate that the microwave heating duration and pH value significantly influenced the fluorescence wavelength and intensity of NCDs. As the duration of radiation increased, the NCDs' PL emission peak location was red-shifted. The PL intensity gradually weakened, and the PL intensity of NCDs under alkaline conditions augmented with the rise of pH and decreased when the pH reached 11. We analyzed the elemental composition and content of their surfaces using XPS. We found that the degree of carbonization and oxygen-containing functional groups contribute significantly to NCDs' PL. The fluorescent properties of NCDs can be efficiently enhanced by increasing the quantity of oxygen-containing functional groups in carbon dots systems, while excessive hydroxyl groups weaken their fluorescence intensity. The intriguing and significant findings of this research can assist us in better grasping the influence of microwave heating duration and pH values on PL properties of NCDs materials. Designing and utilizing the NCDs materials for probe detection can consider these impacts. This study has important scientific significance for effectively regulating the PL emission wavelength of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and improving their fluorescence intensity.

Chunxiao Ren: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Chunlan Tian: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Meng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fengyan Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuzhao Li: Software, Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Fan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Guo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Resources. Ju Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51764052), Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 202301AU070127), and Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Yunnan Province.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
J. Mater. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulation of red-light emission from carbon quantum dots in acid-based environment and the detection of chromium (Ⅲ) ions
J. Mater. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tang, J. Zhang, W.Z. Zhang, et al. Modulation of red-light emission from carbon quantum dots in acid-based environment and the detection of chromium (Ⅲ) ions. J. Mater. Sci. Technol.. 83(2021)58–65. 10.1016/j.jmst.2020.11.072

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots for warm white LEDs
